metadata.title: Why the Fourier Transform Is Just Turning Your Head

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. A quantum state can look narrow in position and broad in momentum without changing at all. The state stays fixed. Only the coordinates used to describe it change.

props.command: How can one quantum state have both a position wavefunction and a momentum wavefunction?
props.segment: Why the Fourier Transform Is Just Turning Your Head
props.runningText: changing basis without changing the ket…
output: The Fourier transform maps the representations.

beats.narration_text: Write the physical state as one ket, psi. Its components in the position basis are psi of x. Its components in the momentum basis are phi of p. These are not two states. They are two coordinate descriptions of one state.

A geometric vector makes the idea familiar. Keep the arrow fixed and rotate the coordinate axes. Its component numbers change even though the arrow does not. Quantum basis changes use the same logic in a complex vector space.

In the position basis, each component answers: what probability amplitude is attached to this location? Squaring its magnitude gives the position probability density. A localized packet has a narrow position profile.

In the momentum basis, each component answers the same kind of question for momentum. Squaring its magnitude gives momentum probability density. The same localized state generally needs a broad range of momentum components.

The Fourier transform is the unitary map between these representations. It does not kick the particle or evolve time. It re-expresses the same ket using momentum basis vectors instead of position basis vectors.

For a minimum-uncertainty Gaussian, the width tradeoff is exact. Make the position Gaussian narrower and the momentum Gaussian becomes broader. Make position broader and momentum narrows. The product of standard deviations stays h-bar over two.

The transform carries phase information as well as magnitude. Two position wavefunctions can have the same position density but different phase gradients, and therefore different momentum distributions. Probability pictures alone do not specify the state.

Because the basis change is unitary, normalization is preserved. Total probability is one in either representation. Inner products and all physical predictions agree when operators and states are transformed consistently.

Turning your head is a metaphor, not a literal rotation in ordinary space. Position and momentum bases are connected by a complex integral transform. The invariant object is the abstract state; the plotted functions are representation-dependent coordinates.

Take a Gaussian with position standard deviation one nanometer. The minimum possible momentum standard deviation is h-bar divided by two nanometers, about five point three times ten to the minus twenty-six kilogram meters per second.

Nothing mysterious happened between the two plots. Position localization requires many momentum basis components, just as a sharp pulse requires many Fourier frequencies. Different shadows reveal different structure in the same object.

Your turn. Halve the position width of a minimum-uncertainty Gaussian. What happens to momentum width? It doubles. The uncertainty product stays h-bar over two, while the two coordinate profiles reshape in opposite directions.

Why is the Fourier transform like turning your head? One quantum ket stays fixed while its position and momentum component lists change. The metaphor is basis change; the exact mathematics is a unitary Fourier transform.
Why the Fourier Transform Is Just Turning Your Head. Liam, in for Bear.

props.title: Why the Fourier Transform Is Just Turning Your Head
